text: Life really does come at you fast, dear
viewer. One minute you're a
multi-millionaire actress who hit the
wall so hard that I'm still not sure who
took more damage from the impact,
bemoning the lack of opportunities for
female directors in Hollywood to pick up
some easy performative victim points.
The next minute, your own $90 million
directorial effort absolutely bombs in
its opening weekend gets obliterated by
critics and literally causes mass
walkouts from audiences who couldn't
believe the gigantic steaming pile of
cat [ __ ] you inflicted on them. The
Bride or to give it THE CORRECT TITLE,
THE BRIDE [screaming]
IS ALREADY A STRONG contender for the
worst movie of 2026. And I don't mean so
far either. I mean, when I compose my
best and worst list at the end of
December, I'm pretty sure this loud,
obnoxious, lurid, poorly written,
horribly acted, and schizophrenically
directed 2 hours of pure narcissistic
incompetence is going to be a top
contender for the drinker's naughty
list. Everything bad that you've heard
about this movie is 100% true and more.
It is one of the most unashamedly
terrible films I've seen since section
31. And apart from soaking away your
troubles in a hot tub full of bleach, it
might be one of the most unpleasant ways
to spend 2 hours of your life that I can
think of. Let's get this [ __ ] over with
quickly because every second wasted on
this thing is a second I'm never going
to get back. The movie opens with a
disjointed monologue from the ghost of
Mary Shelly. Yes, we're deep into
pretentious artouse territory with this
one. complaining about the story of
Frankenstein's bride that she never got
to write because the patriarchy stopped
her from doing it or something.
>> You sure about that?
You sure about that? So, naturally, she
does the sensible thing and projects her
ghost into 1930s Chicago, where she
forcibly takes over the body of a woman
named Ida and immediately starts
spouting more incomprehensible [ __ ]
than a third-wave feminist speaker. Her
indiscretions provoke the anger of a
local mob boss who has her escorted out
of the building by the quickest possible
route, which in this case turns out to
be a speedrun down a flight of stairs.
Oh dear. Probably shouldn't have started
dulging his most intimate secrets in a
crowded room. A meanwhile,
Frankenstein's monster shows up at the
offices of a local scientist who it
turns out was a fan of Frankenstein's
work and has published a number of
papers on the subject of reanimating the
deads, which turns out to be just what
he's looking for. Because just like in
the original book, he's looking for a
female companion to ease his loneliness,
if you catch my drift. Oh, you dirty
monster, you. [laughter]
I mean, I'm not entirely sure why it's
taken him over a hundred years to reach
this conclusion, but whatever. Let's
just not think about that. He's
surprised to find that the scientist in
question is a woman who published under
a man's name because the patriarchy
forced her to do it, even though by the
late 1930s, a number of female
scientists had already achieved
widespread recognition. But let's not
worry about that when the movie has a
message to get across. Science lady is
eventually persuaded to create a bride
for the monster who she names Frank. And
by eventually, I mean after like 3
minutes of screen time, but [ __ ] it.
Let's just keep going. All they need to
make their plan work is a dead body. And
well, I think you can see where this is
leading. Pretty soon, Ida's been jolted
with electricity and reanimated,
complete with the possessed ghost of
Mary Shel on board. And Frank goes to
work trying to seduce her, but she's
like, "Nah, it'll be fine." And decides
to hit a local nightclub instead. Not a
dance hall, mind you, or a jazz bar or a
[ __ ] jitterbug aquarium, but a
straightup nightclub that looks like it
came straight out of 2026, complete with
strobe lighting and modern-day dance
music.
>> That doesn't make sense.
>> Just go with it. But then some of the
local patrons get a bit handsy with the
bride which forces Frank to step in and
save her ungrateful arce by curb
stomping the [ __ ] out of them. And the
whole thing gets caught on camera
because golly g whiz, people are always
bringing massive flashbulb mechanical
cameras on nights out with them. So, the
story devolves into a kind of Bonnie and
Clyde situation after that with Frank
and the bride going on the run across
the country where they start to actually
fall in love with each other, pursued by
the police and the mob boss who saw the
photo of the nightclub fight and is a
little bit surprised to find that Ida's
still alive. And while all this is
happening, the oppressed women of
Chicago start to rise up against the
patriarchy, inspired by a speech the
bride gave at a fancy cocktail party
during a standoff with the police. Oh
man, it's like a shitty knockoff of
Joker. Eventually, both parties track
them down to a drive-in movie theater
where Frank gets killed in the
crossfire. So, the bride takes his body
to science lady to reanimate him. But
she's like, "Nah, it'll be fine." But
then the mob guys break in and shoot the
bride. But that's okay because Mary
Shel's ghost decides to step in and
possess his science lady this time so
she can bring them both back to life and
live happily ever after.
Right.
Jesus [ __ ] Mothering Christ. I have
seen a lot of crappy movies in my time,
but I don't think I've seen many that
were so completely determined to waste
such an insane amount of money and
talent. Just like the monster it's based
around, the bride. SORRY, THE BRIDE.
[screaming]
FEELS LIKE A BUNCH OF disjointed and
partly decomposed body parts awkwardly
stitched together. part gothic romance,
part surrealist comedy, part feminist
empowerment fantasy, part period drama,
and part experimental artouse flick.
It's desperately trying to be a bit of
everything without understanding how or
why these things work, or what it even
hopes to achieve by doing any of it.
This kind of schizophrenic, scattershot
approach would have been just fine if
this was some microbudget film school
project with a bunch of unknown actors.
But let's face it, it's not. This
[ __ ] self-indulgent cinematic
travesty cost $90 million and it boasts
the kind of cast that would be the envy
of most highlevel directors. Christian
Bale, Peter Scarsgard, Jake Gyllenhaal,
and Annette Benning. And it's all for
nothing because the script and direction
are complete dog [ __ ] I mean, what kind
of absolute lunatic signed off on this
one? And why are they still employs?
Christian Bale, for example, is a superb
actor, but holy [ __ ] you would never
know it from this film. He spends about
90% of his screen time yelling every
single line of dialogue at the top of
his lungs like he has no idea what he's
supposed to be doing and just decided
screw it. I'm going to go full [ __ ]
with this one. Annette Benning talks and
acts like she walked in from a
completely different movie. And I can't
say I blame her to be honest. She
probably wishes she was in one. The only
good thing I can say about the
performances is that well Jesse
Buckley's pretty entertaining. Not
because she puts depth and nuance into
her character, but she definitely swings
for the fences, that's for sure. The
emotional tone, style, and
cinematography swerves wildly from
surreal dance numbers to incoherent
monologues to attempts at dark paos and
romance to wannabe edgy action scenes to
the point where I actually started to
wonder if the whole thing was some
bizarre social experiment. The feminist
empowerment overtones of this film are
about as subtle as a rainbow colored
chainsaw and as tired and trite as every
other movie that tries to pull crap like
this in 2026. Honestly, you'd think from
this movie that it was still 2017 and
the idea of women finally venting their
rage, real or imagined, and forcing the
world to hear what they have to say was
some exciting new concepts. Trust me, it
[ __ ] isn't. We've heard everything
they have to say for like a decade now,
whether we wanted it or not. and we've
reached the conclusion that most of it
isn't worth listening to. You know, it's
actually kind of interesting that we've
had two major films based on Mary Shel's
book in the past 6 months. One was a
visually sumptuous, poignant, tragic,
beautifully written, and superbly acted
story of ambition, loss, vengeance, and
hope that treated the source material
with respect, and reverence. The other
was a brash, loud, abrasive, mindless
exercise of ego and vanity that shouts
in your face without saying anything
meaningful and squanders the impressive
talent and resources that were lavished
on it. One of those projects was written
and directed by a man and the other was
The Bride. And if you're looking for an
answer to why more female directors
don't get a chance in Hollywood, Maggie,
you only need lay eyes on your own
creation.
Anyway, that's all I've got for today.
Go away now.
